body_txt: Paparazzi are a particular type of photographers who specialize in (to put it generously) candid shots of celebrities, or (to put it ungenerously) the most unexpected, unflattering, privacy-invading candid shots of celebrities possible. They generally do not care about things like "reasonable right to privacy", "how the subject's life might be affected by the photos", or even "trespassing laws". Paparazzi are a staple of any work of fiction dealing with public figures — especially those aiming to show Celebrity is Overrated . Characters will often grow paranoid about getting caught in a camera ambush. The paparazzi are Acceptable Targets , both given the unsavoury nature of their work, and the fact that they piss off the entertainment industry in particular, it's very rare to see a positive or neutral depiction of one. However, in real life, some celebrities have deliberately set up photos for paparazzi when they want to send a particular message . This can be considered the Evil Counterpart to the Intrepid Reporter . Particularly bad ones fall under Immoral Journalist , especially if they write print articles as well. They may also work for a Strawman News Media outlet (though paps in real life are usually freelancers.) "Papped" has become a verb for being photographed by these people. A popular subject of the paparazzi are celebrities and their dating lives so Celebrity Power Couple and Normie Dates a Celebrity are related. Compare Tabloid Melodrama , which often overlaps, and Groupie Brigade when the invasion comes from fans. Also compare Media Scrum . For some reason, most people who do this wear a Press Hat . If the paparazzi strike at night they may be introduced to the scene with a Blinding Camera Flash . If the paparazzi goes after a celebrity's family, see Familial Fame Frustrations . The Trope Namer is the character Coriolano Paparazzo from the 1960 Italian film La Dolce Vita . Grammatical note: in Italian, "paparazzi" is the plural and the singular is "paparazzo", but in English "paparazzi" is often used as a singular.
